section.title: Our Heavenly Dwelling
section.sectionRationale: Paul carries the hope of chapter four to its climax. If the outer self is wasting away, it is because the earthly tent in which we live is being dismantled, but we have a building from God, an eternal house in heaven. So we groan, longing to be clothed with our heavenly dwelling, that mortality may be swallowed up by life. God has prepared us for this and given the Spirit as a pledge. Therefore we walk by faith, not by sight, content to be away from the body and at home with the Lord, aiming to please Him, since we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ.
section.thesis: When the earthly tent of our body is dismantled we have an eternal dwelling from God; the Spirit is the pledge of that future, so we walk by faith and not by sight, longing to be at home with the Lord, and we aim to please Him because we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the earthly tent and the eternal house in heaven; the Spirit as the pledge that steadies faith over sight; and the confident aim to please the Lord before whose judgment seat we must all appear.

units.label: The tent and the eternal house
units.note: groaning to be clothed with our heavenly dwelling

verses.text: For we know that if the earthly {{skenos|tent}} we live in is dismantled, we have a building from God, an eternal house in heaven, not built by human hands.
For in this tent we groan, longing to be clothed with our heavenly dwelling,
because when we are clothed, we will not be found naked.
For while we are in this tent, we groan under our burdens, because we do not wish to be unclothed but clothed, so that our mortality may be swallowed up by life.

units.label: The Spirit as pledge
units.note: we walk by faith, not by sight

verses.text: And it is God who has prepared us for this very purpose and has given us the {{arrabon|Spirit as a pledge}} of what is to come.
Therefore we are always confident, although we know that while we are at home in the body, we are away from the Lord.
For we {{peripateo|walk}} by faith, not by sight.
We are confident, then, and would prefer to be away from the body and at home with the Lord.
So we aspire to please Him, whether we are at home in this body or away from it.
For we must all appear before the {{bema|judgment seat}} of Christ, that each one may receive his due for the things done in the body, whether good or bad.

skenos.gloss: A tent or temporary dwelling, used as a figure for the mortal body. A tent is portable, fragile, and impermanent, fit for a pilgrim but not a lasting home. Paul contrasts it with a building from God, an eternal house not made by human hands. The imagery recalls Israel's wilderness tabernacle, a temporary tent later replaced by a permanent temple, and dignifies the body while pointing beyond it to the resurrection dwelling.

arrabon.gloss: A down payment, deposit, or pledge that guarantees the full payment to come. In commerce it was earnest money securing a transaction, the first instalment of what is certain to follow. God gives believers the Holy Spirit as this pledge, a real foretaste of the coming inheritance that guarantees the whole. The Spirit already at work in us is God's own guarantee that mortality will be swallowed up by life.

peripateo.gloss: To walk, and by extension to conduct one's whole life. Paul uses walking as a picture of the manner of life, the daily path a person treads. To walk by faith, not by sight, is to order the whole of life by trust in God's unseen promises rather than by what the eyes can measure. It describes a settled way of living, not an occasional act of believing.

bema.gloss: A raised platform or tribunal where a judge or magistrate rendered verdicts, the judgment seat. Every person must appear before the bema of Christ to receive what is due for the things done in the body. For the believer this is not a trial to determine salvation, which is secured in Christ, but an appearing before the Lord that assesses and rewards the life lived. It gives sober weight to how the present life is spent.

basic.context: Paul continues from chapter four, where the outer self is wasting away. He now explains why he does not lose heart in the face of death: the body is only a temporary tent, and when it is taken down the believer has an eternal house from God in heaven. So he groans, not to escape the body but to be further clothed with immortality, and God has given the Spirit as the guarantee of that hope. Confident even in the face of death, Paul aims above all to please the Lord, before whose judgment seat all must appear.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage speaks about death, the intermediate state, and resurrection, and Christians have discussed exactly how the pieces fit. The main thrust is clear: death is not the end for the believer but the doorway to being at home with the Lord and finally clothed with a resurrection body. Read the tent and house imagery as hope in the face of mortality, and verse 10 as the sober motivation to live for Christ now.
basic.originalLanguages: The body is a skenos, a tent, temporary and fragile, set against an eternal house from God. The Spirit is the arrabon, the down payment or pledge, guaranteeing the inheritance to come. We peripateo, walk, by faith, ordering all of life by trust rather than sight. And all must appear before the bema, the judgment seat, of Christ.
basic.typology: The tent recalls the wilderness tabernacle, a temporary dwelling later replaced by a permanent temple, picturing the mortal body giving way to the resurrection body. The longing for mortality to be swallowed up by life echoes the promise that death itself will be defeated, fulfilled in Christ's resurrection and the resurrection of His people.
basic.christ: Christ is the Lord to be at home with, the one whose presence is the believer's true dwelling. His resurrection is the guarantee and pattern of the eternal house not made by hands. He is the judge before whose seat all must appear, and yet for the believer He is the Saviour who has already secured their acceptance, so the judgment seat holds no condemnation but assesses a life lived for Him.
basic.god: God is the builder of the eternal house not made with hands, the one who has prepared His people for immortality and given the Spirit as the pledge of it. He is the God of resurrection hope, who purposes that mortality be swallowed up by life. The whole passage rests on His faithfulness to complete what He has begun.
basic.crossReferences: First Corinthians 15 unfolds the resurrection body that is imperishable. Romans 8 speaks of the whole creation and believers groaning as they await redemption. Philippians 1 expresses the desire to depart and be with Christ. Romans 14 says each will give account before God.
basic.oneStory: The account of redemption moves toward the defeat of death and the gift of resurrection life. Here Paul faces death with hope: the fragile tent of the body will be taken down, but an eternal house awaits, guaranteed by the Spirit. To be away from the body is to be at home with the Lord, and the final hope is not to be unclothed but clothed, mortality swallowed up by life in the resurrection.

context.literary: These verses complete the thought begun in chapter four about the wasting outer self and the eternal weight of glory, and they set up the ministry of reconciliation that follows in verses 11 to 21. The for that opens verse 1 links the eternal house to the unseen realities of chapter four. The passage moves from confidence about the future body, to the Spirit as pledge, to the resolve to please the Lord.
context.historical: Paul, a tentmaker by trade, uses the familiar image of a tent for the mortal body, a natural picture for a travelling apostle who faced constant danger of death. The judgment seat, or bema, was a well known feature of Roman civic life, a raised platform in the marketplace where officials rendered verdicts, an image his readers would immediately recognise.
context.canonical: The chapter develops the resurrection hope of First Corinthians 15 and connects with Paul's longing in Philippians 1 to be with Christ. The reference to the judgment seat stands alongside Romans 14 and other texts on the accountability of all people before God, and it has shaped Christian reflection on death, the intermediate state, and the resurrection body.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To give believers confidence in the face of death by showing that the dismantling of the mortal body opens onto an eternal dwelling from God, guaranteed by the Spirit, and to press the aim of pleasing the Lord in view of the coming judgment seat of Christ.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The teaching about the eternal house and the Spirit as pledge is doctrine to be believed. Walking by faith not by sight and aspiring to please the Lord are prescriptive: they describe how the certainty of the future is to shape present living for every believer.

debates.question: When does the believer receive the heavenly dwelling, at death or at the resurrection?

views.label: At the final resurrection
views.case: Many read the eternal house as the resurrection body received at Christ's return, so that between death and resurrection the believer is with the Lord but awaits the final clothing, consistent with First Corinthians 15.

views.label: Received at or from the moment of death
views.case: Others argue the believer is clothed with the heavenly dwelling immediately at death, avoiding any nakedness, reading verses 1 to 4 to mean there is no gap of unclothed existence.

debates.bearing: Both agree that to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord and that resurrection glory is certain; the passage gives comfort in death whichever way the timing is understood.

debates.question: What is the intermediate state Paul describes as being away from the body and at home with the Lord?

views.label: Conscious presence with Christ
views.case: The historic Christian reading is that at death the believer is consciously present with the Lord, awaiting the resurrection of the body, matching Philippians 1 and the promise to the thief on the cross.

views.label: An unwelcome nakedness to be avoided
views.case: Some stress Paul's reluctance to be unclothed, taking his longing as directed past any intermediate state to the resurrection body itself, so the emphasis falls on the final clothing.

debates.bearing: Either way the believer's hope is sure: death cannot separate from the Lord, and the goal is the resurrection life in which mortality is swallowed up.

debates.question: What is the judgment seat of Christ for the believer?

views.label: An assessment and reward of the believer's life, not a trial for salvation
views.case: Since believers are justified in Christ, the bema evaluates and rewards how the redeemed lived, without placing salvation in doubt, consistent with First Corinthians 3.

views.label: A solemn appearing that includes all people
views.case: Others stress the universal scope, that all must appear and receive their due, holding together sober accountability with the security of those in Christ.

debates.bearing: For the believer the judgment seat is not condemnation but a real accounting that gives weight to present obedience, motivating a life aimed at pleasing the Lord.

study.originalLanguages: Paul pictures the body as a skenos, a tent, fragile and temporary, over against a permanent building from God, an eternal house not made by hands. God has given the arrabon, the down payment or pledge, namely the Holy Spirit, a real first instalment that guarantees the full inheritance of resurrection life. Meanwhile the believer must peripateo, walk, by faith and not by sight, ordering the whole of life by trust in unseen promises. And this life is lived toward the bema, the judgment seat of Christ, where each one will receive his due for what was done in the body, so the hope of glory and the accountability of the redeemed are held together.

typology.fulfillment: The temporary mortal body, echoing the wilderness tabernacle, giving way to the eternal house of the resurrection body.
The firstfruits and guarantee of the coming inheritance, foreshadowing the full redemption of the body.
Fulfilled in the resurrection, when death is finally defeated in Christ, as First Corinthians 15 declares.

study.christ: Christ is the Lord with whom the believer longs to be at home, so that His presence, not merely a place, is heaven's joy. His resurrection is the guarantee and template of the eternal house not made with hands. He is the one before whose judgment seat all must appear, yet for those who are His He is at once Judge and Saviour, so the appearing is not dread but the day when a life lived for Him is assessed and rewarded. To please Him is the aim that orders the whole of life.
study.god: God is the builder and giver of the eternal dwelling, the one who has prepared His people for immortality and sealed them with the Spirit as the pledge of it. He is the God of resurrection, whose settled purpose is that mortality be swallowed up by life. The confidence Paul commends rests entirely on God's faithfulness to finish what He has begun, so that even death cannot frustrate His purpose for His people.

crossReferences.note: The perishable puts on the imperishable and death is swallowed up in victory, matching mortality swallowed up by life.
Believers who have the firstfruits of the Spirit groan as they await the redemption of their bodies.
Paul desires to depart and be with Christ, which is far better, echoing being at home with the Lord.
We will all stand before the judgment seat of God and each give account, parallel to the judgment seat of Christ.

study.oneStory: The Bible's hope runs toward the defeat of death and the gift of resurrection life. Facing the wasting of the body, Paul does not despair: the fragile tent will be taken down, but an eternal house from God awaits, guaranteed already by the Spirit within. To be away from the body is to be at home with the Lord, and the final hope is not a bodiless existence but resurrection, mortality swallowed up by life. So the believer walks by faith and lives to please the Lord who will one day assess and reward.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by the earthly tent that is dismantled?

options.text: The mortal body, which is temporary, over against an eternal house from God.
options.note: Verse 1 contrasts the fragile tent of the body with a permanent building from God.

options.text: The physical church building where they met.
options.note: The tent is a figure for the body, not a meeting place.

options.text: The city of Corinth that would soon fall.
options.note: Paul speaks of the individual body, not a city.

options.text: The old covenant system of worship.
options.note: The tent here is the mortal body, not the old covenant.

quiz.q: Why does Paul call the Spirit a pledge?

options.text: The Spirit is God's down payment guaranteeing the full inheritance of resurrection life to come.
options.note: Verse 5 uses arrabon, a deposit that secures what is certain to follow.

options.text: The Spirit is only a temporary gift until Christ returns.
options.note: The Spirit is a real foretaste and guarantee, not a mere placeholder.

options.text: Believers pledge the Spirit back to God as a promise.
options.note: It is God who gives the Spirit as His pledge to us, not the reverse.

options.text: The Spirit is a symbol with no real presence now.
options.note: The Spirit is genuinely given and at work as the guarantee of glory.

quiz.q: What does it mean to walk by faith, not by sight?

options.text: To order the whole of life by trust in God's unseen promises rather than what the eyes can measure.
options.note: Verse 7 describes a settled way of living governed by faith in unseen realities.

options.text: To make decisions with no thought or wisdom at all.
options.note: Faith is not thoughtlessness; it trusts God's promises rather than mere appearances.

options.text: To expect visible miracles to guide every step.
options.note: It is precisely trusting when we cannot see, not demanding sight.

options.text: To ignore the physical world as unreal.
options.note: Paul affirms the body and resurrection; he is not denying the physical.

quiz.q: What is the judgment seat of Christ for the believer?

options.text: An appearing before the Lord that assesses and rewards the life lived, not a trial that decides salvation.
options.note: Verse 10 speaks of receiving one's due, while salvation rests secure in Christ.

options.text: A trial to determine whether the believer is finally saved.
options.note: Salvation is secured in Christ; the bema assesses the redeemed life.

options.text: A formality with no bearing on how we live now.
options.note: It gives sober weight to present obedience, aiming to please the Lord.
Paul says we must all appear before it.

questions: Paul faces death as the taking down of a tent, with an eternal house waiting. How does that picture change the way you think about your own mortality?
The Spirit is God's pledge guaranteeing the glory to come. When your hope feels thin, how does it help to know you already have a real foretaste of the future?
To be away from the body is to be at home with the Lord. Does your hope of heaven centre on being with Christ Himself, or on something less?
We walk by faith, not by sight. Where are you tempted to live by what you can see rather than by God's unseen promises?
Paul aims to please the Lord because we must all appear before His judgment seat. How would living with that day in view reshape your choices this week?

translationNotes.note: The earthly tent renders skenos, a temporary dwelling used figuratively for the mortal body, set against a permanent building from God, the resurrection dwelling.
The word rendered pledge is arrabon, a commercial down payment guaranteeing full payment; the Spirit is the first instalment and guarantee of the coming inheritance.

The judgment seat renders bema, a raised tribunal; for the believer it is an appearing that assesses and rewards the life lived rather than a trial that determines salvation, which is secured in Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Corinthians 5:1-10. No em dashes were present in this passage; none introduced.
